edges.description: Loss of sacsin perturbs neuronal intermediate filament organization.
Loss of sacsin disrupts neuronal bioenergetic homeostasis.
Loss of sacsin perturbs protein quality control pathways.
Cytoskeletal dysfunction in cerebellar circuits contributes to progressive ataxia.
Long-tract neuronal dysfunction contributes to progressive spasticity.
Cytoskeletal disorganization in sacsin-deficient neurons contributes to the progressive cerebellar ataxia that defines ARSACS.
Motor-circuit dysfunction downstream of sacsin loss explains dysarthria as part of the ARSACS neurological syndrome.
intermediate_mechanisms: Cerebellar and corticobulbar motor circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Cerebellar and ocular-motor circuit involvement produces nystagmus in ARSACS.
Sacsin-deficient cerebellar neurodegeneration is reflected radiographically by cerebellar vermis atrophy.
Chronic bioenergetic stress contributes to peripheral nerve dysfunction.
Bioenergetic stress in sacsin-deficient motor neurons contributes to poor lower-limb motor control and weakness.
intermediate_mechanisms: Peripheral motor neuron and lower-limb motor-circuit dysfunction.

edges.description: Peripheral nerve involvement downstream of sacsin loss is reflected in abnormal motor conduction and denervation findings.
Chronic proteostatic stress and axonal dysfunction contribute to peripheral neuropathy.
Proteostatic stress and axonal dysfunction contribute to distal muscle wasting through the peripheral neuropathy arm.
Progressive combined central and peripheral motor impairment can culminate in loss of independent ambulation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Progressive cerebellar ataxia, spasticity, and peripheral neuropathy.

edges.description: Calcium and lipid dysregulation may amplify neuronal vulnerability, but the precise clinical contribution remains under investigation.

edges.target: Abnormal optic disc and peripapillary retinal nerve fiber morphology

nodes.description: Many affected individuals have whitish peripapillary retinal nerve-fiber changes or retinal nerve fiber layer thickening visible on fundus examination or OCT.
nodes.id: Abnormal optic disc and peripapillary retinal nerve fiber morphology

evidence.reference_title: Inner Retinal Dysfunction in the Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: Most patients with ARSACS have a whitish peripapillary appearance corresponding to a thickening of the peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer.

nodes.description: Electrophysiologic abnormalities support the sensorimotor neuropathy component of ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: Electromyography and nerve conduction studies in Friedreich's ataxia and autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS).
evidence.snippet: Although the clinical evolution was better in the latter, there were more electromyographic signs of denervation and the motor conduction velocities were slower.

nodes.description: Audiology evaluation and hearing-aid planning are appropriate when hearing impairment is present.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS.
evidence.snippet: As needed, services for mild intellectual disability and dysarthria and use of hearing aids.

nodes.description: Proteomic and lipidomic studies of SACS-deficient fibroblasts and neuronal cells indicate that calcium and lipid homeostasis are perturbed downstream of sacsin loss.

evidence.reference_title: Proteomics and lipidomic analysis reveal dysregulated pathways associated with loss of sacsin.
evidence.snippet: DISCUSSION: In addition to confirming aberrant Ca2+ homeostasis in ARSACS, this study described abnormal lipid levels associated with loss of sacsin.

nodes.description: Cerebellar vermis atrophy is a characteristic neuroimaging finding in ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: Inner Retinal Dysfunction in the Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: Magnetic resonance imaging revealed atrophy of the cerebellar vermis (Figure 4) and symmetrical linear hypointense striations on either side of the pontine midline on T1-weighted sequences (Figure 4).

nodes.description: Sacsin deficiency disrupts neuronal cytoskeletal organization and is associated with abnormal neurofilament aggregation, a recurring cellular signature of ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Organelle-Based Functional Proteomics: In Silico Prediction of Impaired Functional Annotations in SACS KO Cell Model.
evidence.snippet: Our integrated results strengthen the evidence for disease-specific defects related to bioenergetics and protein quality control systems and reinforce the role of dysregulated cytoskeletal organization in the pathogenesis of ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: In Vitro Characterization of Motor Neurons and Purkinje Cells Differentiated from Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells Generated from Patients with Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: In addition, characteristic neurofilament aggregates were detected along the neurites of both iPSC-derived neurons.

nodes.description: Distal muscle wasting can develop as part of the peripheral neuropathy phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Diplomyelia in a patient with a clinical suspicion of autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay type (ARSACS).
evidence.snippet: Characteristic clinical features are ataxia, spasticity, distal muscle wasting, neuropathy, dysarthria, nystagmus, and finger or feet deformities.

nodes.description: Dysarthria is a common speech manifestation in ARSACS and may progress more slowly than lower-limb impairment.

evidence.reference_title: Diplomyelia in a patient with a clinical suspicion of autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay type (ARSACS).
evidence.snippet: Characteristic clinical features are ataxia, spasticity, distal muscle wasting, neuropathy, dysarthria, nystagmus, and finger or feet deformities.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar ataxia is a defining motor phenotype of ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Organelle-Based Functional Proteomics: In Silico Prediction of Impaired Functional Annotations in SACS KO Cell Model.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS) is an inherited neurodegenerative disease characterized by early-onset spasticity in the lower limbs, axonal-demyelinating sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy, and cerebellar ataxia.

nodes.description: Hearing loss is reported in some individuals with ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS.
evidence.snippet: Mild intellectual disability, hearing loss, and urinary urgency and incontinence have been reported in some individuals.

nodes.description: Loss of sacsin perturbs protein quality control pathways, contributing to chronic neuronal stress.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Organelle-Based Functional Proteomics: In Silico Prediction of Impaired Functional Annotations in SACS KO Cell Model.
evidence.snippet: Our integrated results strengthen the evidence for disease-specific defects related to bioenergetics and protein quality control systems and reinforce the role of dysregulated cytoskeletal organization in the pathogenesis of ARSACS.

nodes.description: Progressive lower-limb and gait impairment can culminate in wheelchair dependence.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS in the Dutch population: a frequent cause of early-onset cerebellar ataxia.
evidence.snippet: At the time of the clinical examination, the majority (n = 13) of the patients were permanently wheelchair-dependent.

nodes.description: Poor lower-limb motor control and weakness contribute to gait impairment and progressive disability.

evidence.reference_title: In Vitro Characterization of Motor Neurons and Purkinje Cells Differentiated from Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells Generated from Patients with Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS) is an early-onset neurodegenerative disease mainly characterized by spasticity in the lower limbs and poor muscle control.

nodes.description: Loss of sacsin is associated with mitochondrial defects and impaired bioenergetic homeostasis, contributing to progressive neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Organelle-Based Functional Proteomics: In Silico Prediction of Impaired Functional Annotations in SACS KO Cell Model.
evidence.snippet: Our integrated results strengthen the evidence for disease-specific defects related to bioenergetics and protein quality control systems and reinforce the role of dysregulated cytoskeletal organization in the pathogenesis of ARSACS.

nodes.description: Nystagmus and other ocular motor disturbances are recognized ARSACS manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Diplomyelia in a patient with a clinical suspicion of autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay type (ARSACS).
evidence.snippet: Characteristic clinical features are ataxia, spasticity, distal muscle wasting, neuropathy, dysarthria, nystagmus, and finger or feet deformities.

nodes.description: Ophthalmology evaluation, including OCT when available, can document characteristic peripapillary retinal nerve-fiber-layer abnormalities and assess visual comorbidities.

evidence.reference_title: Inner Retinal Dysfunction in the Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: The autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS) is associated with structural retinal abnormalities either directly visible on funduscopy or revealed by optical coherence tomography (OCT).

nodes.description: Axonal-demyelinating sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy is a major component of the ARSACS phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Integrative Organelle-Based Functional Proteomics: In Silico Prediction of Impaired Functional Annotations in SACS KO Cell Model.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS) is an inherited neurodegenerative disease characterized by early-onset spasticity in the lower limbs, axonal-demyelinating sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy, and cerebellar ataxia.

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar ataxia is the central neurologic feature and can begin in early childhood.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS.
evidence.snippet: Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay (ARSACS) is clinically characterized by a progressive cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, and spasticity.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of a novel SACS gene mutation leading to spastic ataxia Charlevoix-Saguenay type: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Exome sequencing analysis revealed two compound heterozygous variants in the sacsin molecular chaperone gene, one of which was novel.

nodes.description: ARSACS is caused by biallelic SACS variants that reduce or abolish sacsin function, establishing the primary molecular lesion in the disorder.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of a novel SACS gene mutation leading to spastic ataxia Charlevoix-Saguenay type: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Exome sequencing analysis revealed two compound heterozygous variants in the sacsin molecular chaperone gene, one of which was novel.

evidence.reference_title: In Vitro Characterization of Motor Neurons and Purkinje Cells Differentiated from Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells Generated from Patients with Autosomal Recessive Spastic Ataxia of Charlevoix-Saguenay.
evidence.snippet: The disease is caused by mutations in the SACS gene leading in most cases to a loss of function of the sacsin protein, which is highly expressed in motor neurons and Purkinje cells.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS in the Dutch population: a frequent cause of early-onset cerebellar ataxia.
evidence.snippet: All mutations most likely lead to a loss of function.

nodes.description: Lower-limb spasticity is one of the classic cardinal features of ARSACS.

evidence.reference_title: Identification of a novel SACS gene mutation leading to spastic ataxia Charlevoix-Saguenay type: a case report.
evidence.snippet: BACKGROUND: Spastic ataxia Charlevoix-Saguenay is a rare autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by a combination of spasticity, ataxia, and peripheral neuropathy.

nodes.description: Oral antispasticity medication such as baclofen can be used symptomatically in early disease to manage spasticity and reduce secondary contracture risk.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS.
evidence.snippet: physical therapy and oral medications such as baclofen to control spasticity in the early phase of the disease may prevent tendon shortening and joint contractures and, hence, may help to postpone major functional disabilities until severe muscle weakness or cerebellar ataxia occur.

nodes.description: Speech-language therapy and related communication support are appropriate for dysarthria and other speech/swallow issues when present.

evidence.reference_title: ARSACS.
evidence.snippet: As needed, services for mild intellectual disability and dysarthria and use of hearing aids.
